content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 7, 1994

Vanessa Baird, Chief
CARE Section
Department of Health Services
714/744 P Street
P.O. Box 942732
Sacramento, CA  94234-7320


Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-299

Dear Ms. Baird:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of the members of the Title II HIV Comprehensive Care
Working Group under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTIONS

     1.     Are the members of the Title II HIV Comprehensive Care
Working Group (the "Working Group") "public officials" subject to
the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act?

     2.     Is the Working Group subject to the conflict of interest
code requirements?

CONCLUSIONS

     1.     As members of a decisionmaking board or commission of
the State Department of Health Services, all of the Working Group
members are "public officials" as defined by the Act.
Consequently, the members are subject to the disclosure and
disqualification requirements of the Act.

     2.     The Working Group must comply with the requirements of
Sections 87300 to 87313.

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-94-299
Page 2


FACTS

The Title II HIV Comprehensive Care Working Group operates
under the auspices of Title II of the federal Ryan White
Comprehensive AIDS Resources Emergency (CARE) Act of 1990, passed
by Congress to provide health care and support services to people
living with HIV and AIDS. Title II provides federal grants to
states according to a formula based on the number of reported AIDS
cases in each state.

The Department of Health Services, Office of Aids (DHS/OA),
as the grantee for California's Title II funds, is responsible for
the planning, administration, and evaluation of the CARE Act
programs.

The Working Group is comprised of individuals invited to
provide recommendations to DHS/OA on distribution of resources
received under Title II, broad policy issues, and priorities for
services to targeted populations. DHS/OA has convened meetings of
the Working Group since 1990 to obtain recommendations for Title
II funding and programs and has generally adopted those
recommendations each year without significant amendment or
modification. Working Group members include nonvoting advisors
and voting members.

The nonvoting advisors are individuals invited to provide
recommendations and guidance to DHS/OA on who should serve as
voting members of the Working Group and on development of agendas
for the working Group meetings. They also act as advisors/
facilitators to the Working Group by participating in all
discussions and lending their experience and expertise to the
Working Group's deliberations. Advisors being information to the
Working Group and deliver information from the working Group back
to communities and constituencies. This group is comprised of
individuals from federal, state, and local government, service
providers, and advocacy organizations.

Voting members of the Working Group are individuals invited
to provide recommendations to DHS/OA on distribution of Title II
resources, broad policy issues, and priorities for services to
targeted populations. This group is comprised of individuals from
state and local government, service providers, advocacy
organizations, and affected individuals. Similar to advisors,
voting members provide a communication network between the Working
Group and communities and constituents. Unlike advisors, only
voting members can vote on recommendations made to DHS/OA.

ANALYSIS

The General Rule

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making, or attempting to use his or her official

Our File No. A-94-299
Page 3


position to influence a governmental decision if the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official, or on a member of the official's immediate family, or on
any of the official's economic interests.

Public Official

A public official includes every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a local or state
government agency. (Section 82048.) A "public official" includes
members of boards and commissions2 and is defined in Regulation
18700(a)(1) as follows:

     (a)  "Public official at any level of state or
local government" means a member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency. The term "public official" also
includes individuals who perform the same or
substantially the same duties as an individual
holding an office or a position listed in
Government Code Section 87200, including "other
public officials who manage public investments" as
that term is defined in 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18720.

     (1)  "Member" shall include, but not be
limited to, salaried or unsalaried members of
boards or commissions with decisionmaking
authority. A board or commission possesses
decisionmaking authority whenever:

          (A)  It may make a final governmental deci-
sion;

          (B)  It may compel, a governmental decision; or
it may prevent a governmental decision either by
reason of an exclusive power to initiate the deci-
sion or by reason of a veto which may not be over-
ridden; or

          (C)  It makes substantive recommendations
which are, and over an extended period of time have




2  This would include task forces, committees, and other advisory
groups. (See e.g., Snow Advice Letter, No. I-90-728; Williams
Advice Letter, No. A-92-135; Woodbury Advice Letter, No. A-90-
665.)

Our File No. A-94-299
Page 4


been, regularly approved without significant amendment or modification by another public official or
governmental agency.

(Emphasis added.)

Accordingly, if the Working Group meets any of the tests of
Regulation 18700(a)(1)(A), (B) or (C), it possesses decision-
making authority, and its members are deemed public officials
subject to the conflict of interest requirements of the Act.

Regulation 18700(a)(1)(C) seeks to include within the scope
of the conflict of interest provisions of the Act those
commissions and boards whose recommendations are merely "rubber
stamped" by a public official or governmental agency. Therefore,
if a pattern develops whereby the recommendations of a board or
commission are regularly approved without substantive modification
and review, it is likely that the board or commission would become
a decisionmaking body for purposes of the Act. (Czach Advice
Letter, No. A-91-503.)

a.     Substantive Recommendations

Based on the information provided, the Working Group makes
substantive recommendations to the Department of Health Services,
Office of Aids, which have been regularly approved over an
extended period of time. The recommendations of both nonvoting
advisors and voting members are substantive. For example,
although only voting members can formally vote on recommendations
to DHS/OA, Working Group members generally make recommendations on
distribution of resources received under Title II, broad policy
issues, and priorities for services to targeted populations.
Clearly, nonvoting advisors influence those decisions by
participating in the discussions. For example, according to your
facts, they also act as advisors/facilitators to the Working Group
by participating in all discussions and lending their experience
and expertise to the Working Group's deliberations.

In addition, the nonvoting advisors provide recommendations
and guidance to DHS/OA on the composition of the voting members of
the Working Group and on the development of agendas for the
Working Group meetings.

b.     No Significant Amendment or Modification

According to a telephone conversation with Ms. Lisette
Willmarth on October 3, 1994, the DHS/OA adopts most of the
recommendations (99%) of the Working Group each year without
significant amendment or modification.

The Commission has concluded on several occasions that where
an advisory committee makes substantive recommendations to a
governmental agency that are regularly approved without
significant modification, then the members are public officials

Our File No. A-94-299
Page 5


under the Act and they are subject to the disclosure and
disqualification provisions. (Regulation 18700(a)(1)(C); also,
see Woodbury Advice Letter, A-90-665; Paley Advice Letter, A-90-
583; Korade Advice Letter, A-89-715.)

According to your facts, the Working Group's recommendations
are substantive and have been regularly approved for approximately
four years without significant amendment or modification by
another public official or governmental agency. Therefore, there
is a history of the Working Group's recommendations being
routinely accepted without amendment or modification, and the
Working Group has been converted from a solely advisory group to
one making or participating in the making of governmental
decisions. (Bali Advice Letter, No. I-89-671.)

Conflicts of Interest

Since the Working Group members are members of a
decisionmaking board or commission, they are "public officials"
within the meaning of Section 87100. Therefore, the
members³ are subject to the disclosure and disqualification
provisions of the Act.

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. Therefore, the members may
not make, participate in making or influence any governmental
decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable and material
financial effect on the official's economic interests as defined
in Section 87103.

Section 87103 provides that an official has a financial
interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable⁴ that the decision will have a


_________________________
3 Please note, however, that your facts indicate that both
nonvoting advisors and voting members are members of the same
Working Group. Thus, we have not treated the nonvoting advisors
as a separate group.

4 Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Com. (1987) 196
Cal.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Our File No. A-94-299
Page 6


material⁵ financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on the official or a member of his or her
immediate family, or on the following economic interests:

       (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

       (b)  Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

       (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

       (d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

       (e)  Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

       For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouses, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.


_________________________
5    The Commission has adopted several regulations on the subject
of material financial effect. (Regulations 18702.1 - 18702.6.)
The members should consult these regulations or seek additional
guidance from the Commission with respect to a specific
governmental decision.

Our File No. A-94-299
Page 7


In addition, Regulation 18700.1(a) provides:

     With regard to a governmental decision which
is within or before an official's agency or an
agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency, the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or
appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency.  Attempts to influence include, but are not
limited to, appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer.

                                    (Emphasis added.)

     Under Regulation 18700.1, a member would be attempting to
influence his or her own agency (the Department of Health
Services) by participating as a member of the Working Group in
making recommendations to the DHS/OA.

     Thus, when a Working Group member has a financial interest in
a decision, the official is disqualified from participating in the
Working Group's recommendations regarding the decision. (Furth
Advice Letter No. I-87-079; Setter Advice Letter, No. I-92-169.)
For example, the member would be prohibited from voting on or
participating in the decisions if his or her employer was being
considered for a grant.  Therefore, the official would be
prohibited from participating in discussions, or making
recommendations as a member of the Working Group, in order to
influence governmental decisions concerning the granting of monies
to his or her employer.

Conflict of Interest Code

     Section 87300 provides that every agency shall adopt and
promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to the provisions
of this article. "Agency" means any state agency or local
government agency (Section 82003.)  "State agency" means every
state office, department, divisions, bureau, board and commission,
and the legislature. (Section 82049.) Therefore, the Working
Group is a state agency which must comply with Section 87300 by
adopting its own code.


_________________________

6    Section 87301 also provides it is the policy of the Act that
Conflict of Interest Codes shall be formulated at the most
decentralized level possible, but without precluding intra-
departmental review.

Our File No. A-94-299
Page 8


     In addition, Section 87302 requires that every conflict of
interest code must specifically enumerate the positions within the
agency which involve the making or participation in the making of
decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any
financial interest.  This would include the nonvoting advisors,
who participate in the making of governmental decisions.

     The conflict of interest code must contain requirements that
each designated employee must file statements disclosing
reportable investments, business positions, interests in real
property, and income. (Section 87302(b).) Finally, the conflict
of interest code must state the specific types of investments,
business positions, interests in real property, and sources of
income which are reportable. (Section 87302(a).)

     The conflict of interest code must be submitted to the code-
reviewing body within six months after the agency comes into
existence for the code-reviewing body's approval. (Section
87303.) Section 82011 provides that the code-reviewing body is
the Commission with respect to the conflict of interest code of a
state agency. Consequently, when the Working Group develops its
conflict of interest code it must submit the conflict of interest
code to the Commission for approval.

     Once a conflict of interest code has been developed for the
Working Group, members will be required to file an assuming office
statement 30 days after the effective date of the conflict of
interest code.

     We have enclosed general information concerning conflict of
interest codes. If you have any further questions regarding
conflict of interest code requirements, please contact the
Division of Technical Assistance at (916) 322-5662 or, if you have
other questions please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

                                   Sincerely,


                                   Steven G. Churchwell
                                   General Counsel

                                   [signature: Luisa Menchaca]

                                   By:  Luisa Menchaca
                                        Counsel, Legal Division


Enclosures

     7 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

Section 87300 provides that every agency shall adopt and
promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to the provisions
of this article.
6

government agency (Section 82003.)
“State agency” means every
state office, department, divisions, bureau, board and commission,

(Section 82049.)

Group is a state agency which must comply with Section 87300 by

who participate in the making of governmental decisions.
The conflict of interest code must contain requirements that
each designated employee must file statements disclosing

property,
and income.
(Section 87302 (b).)
Finally, the conflict
of interest code must state the specific types of investments,
business positions, interests in real property,
and sources of
income which are reportable.
(Section 87302(a).)
The conflict of interest code must be submitted to the code—
reviewing body within six months after the agency comes into
existence for the code—reviewing body’s approval.
(Section
87303.)
Section 82011 provides that the code—reviewing body is
the Commission with respect to the conflict of interest code of a
state agency.
Consequently,
when the Working Group develops its
conflict of interest code it must submit the conflict of interest
code to the Commission for approval.
Once a conflict of interest code has been developed for the
Working Group,
members will be required to file an assuming office
statement 30 days after the effective date of the conflict of
interest code.
We have enclosed general information concerning conflict of
interest codes.

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission October 7, 1994 Vanessa Baird, Chief CARE Section Department of Health Services 714/744 P Street P.O. Box 942732 Sacramento, CA 94234-7320 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-94-299 Dear Ms. Baird: This is in response to your letter requesting advice on behalf of the members of the Title II HIV Comprehensive Care Working Group under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 QUESTIONS 1. Are the members of the Title II HIV Comprehensive Care Working Group (the "Working Group") "public officials" subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act? 2. Is the Working Group subject to the conflict of interest code requirements? CONCLUSIONS 1. As members of a decisionmaking board or commission of the State Department of Health Services, all of the Working Group members are "public officials" as defined by the Act. Consequently, the members are subject to the disclosure and disqualification requirements of the Act. 2. The Working Group must comply with the requirements of Sections 87300 to 87313. _______________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-94-299 Page 2 FACTS The Title II HIV Comprehensive Care Working Group operates under the auspices of Title II of the federal Ryan White Comprehensive AIDS Resources Emergency (CARE) Act of 1990, passed by Congress to provide health care and support services to people living with HIV and AIDS. Title II provides federal grants to states according to a formula based on the number of reported AIDS cases in each state. The Department of Health Services, Office of Aids (DHS/OA), as the grantee for California's Title II funds, is responsible for the planning, administration, and evaluation of the CARE Act programs. The Working Group is comprised of individuals invited to provide recommendations to DHS/OA on distribution of resources received under Title II, broad policy issues, and priorities for services to targeted populations. DHS/OA has convened meetings of the Working Group since 1990 to obtain recommendations for Title II funding and programs and has generally adopted those recommendations each year without significant amendment or modification. Working Group members include nonvoting advisors and voting members. The nonvoting advisors are individuals invited to provide recommendations and guidance to DHS/OA on who should serve as voting members of the Working Group and on development of agendas for the working Group meetings. They also act as advisors/ facilitators to the Working Group by participating in all discussions and lending their experience and expertise to the Working Group's deliberations. Advisors being information to the Working Group and deliver information from the working Group back to communities and constituencies. This group is comprised of individuals from federal, state, and local government,
